# Roadmap-MOC Home Note — the two-zone structure

The roadmap-MOC "home note" is the single navigable map for the whole spec
tree. `speckit-prd` writes it at `docs/ai/specs/<slug>-roadmap-MOC.md` alongside
the PRD and the technical roadmap, and `speckit-status` reads the roadmap to
report progress. This guide explains how the file is structured so you know
which parts you may edit by hand and which parts you must never touch.

## Contents

- [The two zones](#the-two-zones)
- [Zone 1 — the curated epics zone (hand-edited)](#zone-1--the-curated-epics-zone-hand-edited)
- [Zone 2 — the GENERATED INDEX zone (never hand-edited)](#zone-2--the-generated-index-zone-never-hand-edited)
- [The ~10-epic cap is advisory](#the-10-epic-cap-is-advisory)
- [Why the split exists](#why-the-split-exists)

## The two zones

A roadmap-MOC home note carries exactly **two body zones**, and the line that
separates them — who owns each zone — is the single most important thing to
understand about the file:

| Zone | Who writes it | Edit by hand? |
|------|---------------|---------------|
| **Curated epics zone** | a human (auto-seeded once from the roadmap phases) | **Yes** — this is yours to shape |
| **GENERATED INDEX zone** | the `generate-spec-index.sh` generator | **No** — regenerated every run |

Confusing the two is the one mistake to coach against: hand-editing the
generated zone wastes effort (your edit is overwritten on the next generator
run), and treating the curated zone as machine-owned leaves it as a bare
scaffold that never gets the human judgment it exists to capture.

## Zone 1 — the curated epics zone (hand-edited)

The curated epics zone is **hand-authored and editable**. `speckit-prd` seeds it
**once**, at emission time, as a scaffold derived from the roadmap's existing
phase/tier grouping — one epic per phase, the phase's member spec links, and a
one-line advisory "Why" placeholder per epic. It adds **zero** new interview
questions to do this; the grouping already exists in the roadmap.

From that point on the zone belongs to the author. Edit it freely: rename epics,
regroup specs, replace each "Why" placeholder with the real rationale, add or
remove epics as the roadmap evolves. The generator never reads or touches this
zone, so your edits are safe across regenerations.

When the roadmap has no phase grouping (a flat catalog), the scaffold collapses
to a single "Specs" epic listing all specs, plus an advisory note suggesting you
group them — still entirely yours to edit.

## Zone 2 — the GENERATED INDEX zone (never hand-edited)

The GENERATED INDEX zone is **machine-regenerated and must never be hand-edited**.
It is produced deterministically by the shared `generate-spec-index.sh` generator
and is bounded by a sentinel pair whose comment says so out loud:

```text
<!-- GENERATED:INDEX:START (do not edit; regenerated by generate-spec-index.sh) -->
- [PRSG-001](../../../specs/prsg-001-foo/SPEC-MOC.md) · complete
- [PRSG-002](../../../specs/prsg-002-bar/SPEC-MOC.md) · in-progress
<!-- GENERATED:INDEX:END -->
```

Key facts to convey:

- **One row per in-scope spec**, ordered normalized-ID ascending. Each row is
  exactly `- [spec_id](rel/SPEC-MOC.md) · status`, where `spec_id` and `status`
  are read from that spec's `SPEC-MOC.md` frontmatter and the separator is the
  U+00B7 middle dot.
- **Every link is a relative `[]()` Markdown target** resolving to the spec's
  `SPEC-MOC.md`, never a `[[wikilink]]` — so every spec is reachable from the
  home note.
- **Whole-zone regeneration.** Everything between the two sentinels is rewritten
  on every generator run, so the INDEX is staleness-free and a second run with
  no intervening changes is a byte-identical, zero-diff no-op.
- **Hand edits are lost.** Anything you type between the sentinels is discarded
  the next time the generator runs. To change a row, change the source — the
  spec's `SPEC-MOC.md` frontmatter — not the INDEX.

Do not move, rename, or delete the sentinel comment lines either: the generator
finds the zone by matching those exact bytes.

## The ~10-epic cap is advisory

The "cap epics below ~10" guideline is **advisory**. Nothing here blocks, gates,
or rejects a home note. When the auto-derived scaffold yields more than about ten
epics, `speckit-prd` prints a single one-line advisory (for example, "11 epics —
consider consolidating; more than ~10 strains navigability") and **still writes
the file**. It is a navigability nudge, never a hard stop, never a CI lint, never
an exit code.

Coach the guideline as a prompt to consolidate, not a rule to satisfy: a map with
a dozen-plus top-level epics is harder to scan, so collapsing closely related
epics usually helps — but the author always decides, and the file is written
regardless.

## Why the split exists

The split lets one document do two jobs that a single hand-maintained list does
badly. The **curated zone** captures human judgment — which specs belong
together, and why — that no generator can infer. The **GENERATED INDEX** captures
the exhaustive, always-current, machine-verifiable list of every spec, so nothing
is silently dropped and the index never goes stale. Hand-authoring the index
would rot; generating the curation would erase the judgment. Keeping them in
separate, clearly-owned zones is what makes the home note both trustworthy and
navigable.
